log_shift.name: Log-shift
log_shift.physical_motivation: Log transformation is the standard variance-stabilising transform in hydrology for streamflow, precipitation and flood frequency data with right-skewed distributions.
log_shift.canonical_use_case: Used pervasively in flood frequency analysis (Vogel & Wilson 1996) and log-Pearson Type III fitting.
log_shift.reference_full: Vogel, R. M., & Wilson, I. (1996). Probability distribution of annual maximum, mean, and minimum streamflows in the United States. Journal of Hydrologic Engineering, 1(2), 69–76.
log_shift.notes: Strongly concave: compresses high-valued outputs. Per-sample shift makes it nonlocal (depends on min(Y)). Sobol indices of high-sensitivity inputs may be attenuated in the upper tail.

params_description.eps: Small positive shift to ensure log-argument is positive.

power_law_beta2.name: Power-law (β=2, depth-damage)
power_law_beta2.physical_motivation: Depth-damage curves in flood risk assessment relate inundation depth to property damage using a power law with exponent near 2.
power_law_beta2.canonical_use_case: Applied in flood damage modelling (Merz et al. 2010, Nat. Hazards Earth Syst. Sci.) to convert a depth field to economic loss.
power_law_beta2.reference_full: Merz, B., Kreibich, H., Schwarze, R., & Thieken, A. (2010). Review article: Assessment of economic flood damage. Nat. Hazards Earth Syst. Sci., 10, 1697–1724.
power_law_beta2.notes: Quadratic amplification of high values. Strongly shifts sensitivity towards inputs governing the highest output region. Normalisation is nonlocal.

params_description.beta: Power-law exponent; beta=2 gives quadratic (depth-damage); beta>1 convex; beta<1 concave.

power_law_beta05.name: Power-law (β=0.5, square-root)
power_law_beta05.physical_motivation: Square-root (beta=0.5) variance-stabilising transform for count-like hydrological quantities (runoff coefficients).
power_law_beta05.canonical_use_case: Variance stabilisation for Poisson-distributed or right-skewed count data in hydrology.
power_law_beta05.reference_full: Anscombe, F. J. (1948). The transformation of Poisson, binomial and negative-binomial data. Biometrika, 35(3–4), 246–254.
power_law_beta05.notes: Concave: compresses high values relative to low. Reverses the power-law amplification. Reduces sensitivity of high-variance inputs.

params_description.beta: Power-law exponent; beta<1 gives concave/compressive transform.

box_cox_sqrt.name: Box-Cox (λ=0.5)
box_cox_sqrt.physical_motivation: Box-Cox power transformation family for normalising right-skewed distributions in environmental data.
box_cox_sqrt.canonical_use_case: Standard preprocessing in regression/sensitivity analysis of environmental model outputs to satisfy Gaussian residual assumptions.
box_cox_sqrt.reference_full: Box, G. E. P., & Cox, D. R. (1964). An analysis of transformations. J. Roy. Stat. Soc. B, 26(2), 211–252.
box_cox_sqrt.notes: MLE-optimal normality transform for lambda=0.5. Concave for positive values. Shift and scale are nonlocal.

params_description.lam: Box-Cox lambda; lambda=0 gives log; lambda=1 gives identity.

box_cox_log.name: Box-Cox (λ→0, log limit)
box_cox_log.physical_motivation: Log limit of Box-Cox; equivalent to log(y+1) after per-sample minimum shift. Standard normalisation for positive data.
box_cox_log.canonical_use_case: Log transformation used throughout environmental SA to reduce skewness before variance decomposition.
box_cox_log.reference_full: Box, G. E. P., & Cox, D. R. (1964). An analysis of transformations. J. Roy. Stat. Soc. B, 26(2), 211–252.
box_cox_log.notes: Limiting case of Box-Cox; commutes with affine preprocessing. Strong concavity implies sensitivity mass migrates to low-value inputs.

params_description.lam: Lambda parameter; lam~0 gives log transform.

clipped_excess_q90.name: Clipped excess POT (q=0.90)
clipped_excess_q90.physical_motivation: Peaks-over-threshold (POT) transform retains only values exceeding the 90th percentile; standard in extreme value theory for flood/storm modelling.
clipped_excess_q90.canonical_use_case: Extreme value SA: identifies which inputs drive extreme events above a design threshold.
clipped_excess_q90.reference_full: Coles, S. (2001). An Introduction to Statistical Modeling of Extreme Values. Springer.
clipped_excess_q90.notes: Non-smooth at the threshold; destroys sensitivity information below Q90. Sobol indices computed on this output characterise extreme-event drivers.

exceedance_q75.name: Exceedance indicator (q=0.75)
exceedance_q75.physical_motivation: Binary exceedance indicator; reduces a continuous field to 0/1 event occurrence. Standard in flood-frequency and climate-extreme SA.
exceedance_q75.canonical_use_case: Identifies which model inputs drive the probability of exceeding a design threshold.
exceedance_q75.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer. Wiley.
exceedance_q75.notes: Discontinuous. Collapses all metric information to binary. Sobol indices on Bernoulli output measure probability-of-exceedance sensitivity.

exceedance_q90.name: Exceedance indicator (q=0.90)
exceedance_q90.physical_motivation: Binary exceedance indicator; reduces a continuous field to 0/1 event occurrence. Standard in flood-frequency and climate-extreme SA.
exceedance_q90.canonical_use_case: Identifies which model inputs drive the probability of exceeding a design threshold.
exceedance_q90.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer. Wiley.
exceedance_q90.notes: Discontinuous. Collapses all metric information to binary. Sobol indices on Bernoulli output measure probability-of-exceedance sensitivity.

exceedance_q95.name: Exceedance indicator (q=0.95)
exceedance_q95.physical_motivation: Binary exceedance indicator; reduces a continuous field to 0/1 event occurrence. Standard in flood-frequency and climate-extreme SA.
exceedance_q95.canonical_use_case: Identifies which model inputs drive the probability of exceeding a design threshold.
exceedance_q95.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer. Wiley.
exceedance_q95.notes: Discontinuous. Collapses all metric information to binary. Sobol indices on Bernoulli output measure probability-of-exceedance sensitivity.

exceedance_q99.name: Exceedance indicator (q=0.99)
exceedance_q99.physical_motivation: Binary exceedance indicator; reduces a continuous field to 0/1 event occurrence. Standard in flood-frequency and climate-extreme SA.
exceedance_q99.canonical_use_case: Identifies which model inputs drive the probability of exceeding a design threshold.
exceedance_q99.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer. Wiley.
exceedance_q99.notes: Discontinuous. Collapses all metric information to binary. Sobol indices on Bernoulli output measure probability-of-exceedance sensitivity.

gumbel_cdf.name: Gumbel CDF (EV type-I)
gumbel_cdf.physical_motivation: Gumbel (EV Type I) distribution CDF used in annual maximum flood / wind speed frequency analysis.
gumbel_cdf.canonical_use_case: Transforms a simulated extreme-value field to non-exceedance probability for comparison with observations.
gumbel_cdf.reference_full: Gumbel, E. J. (1958). Statistics of Extremes. Columbia University Press.
gumbel_cdf.notes: Double-exponential; strong nonlinearity in both tails. Parameters estimated per-sample (nonlocal). Inflection at mode.

frechet_cdf.name: Fréchet CDF (EV type-II)
frechet_cdf.physical_motivation: Fréchet (EV Type II) CDF for heavy-tailed maxima such as ocean wave heights and insurance losses.
frechet_cdf.canonical_use_case: Heavy-tail normalisation for right-skewed maxima with infinite variance.
frechet_cdf.reference_full: Fréchet, M. (1927). Sur la loi de probabilité de l'écart maximum. Ann. Soc. Polonaise Math., 6, 93–116.
frechet_cdf.notes: Shape parameter alpha controls tail heaviness. Normalised y via per-sample min/range (nonlocal).

params_description.shape: Tail shape parameter alpha; smaller = heavier tail.

log_normal_cdf.name: Log-normal CDF (σ=0.5)
log_normal_cdf.physical_motivation: Log-normal CDF used to model probability of non-exceedance for precipitation, streamflow and groundwater.
log_normal_cdf.canonical_use_case: Standard transformation in hydrology for converting simulated depths to return-period probabilities.
log_normal_cdf.reference_full: Limpert, E., Stahel, W. A., & Abbt, M. (2001). Log-normal distributions across the sciences. BioScience, 51(5), 341–352.
log_normal_cdf.notes: Parameters estimated per-sample via log-mean and sigma (nonlocal). Smooth S-shaped CDF.

params_description.sigma: Log-normal shape parameter sigma (standard deviation of log Y).

return_period.name: Return period T=1/(1-F)
return_period.physical_motivation: Return period transforms a simulated field to the expected recurrence interval; used in flood design standards (100-year, 500-year events).
return_period.canonical_use_case: SA on return-period outputs identifies inputs that control the magnitude of rare events.
return_period.reference_full: Coles, S. (2001). An Introduction to Statistical Modeling of Extreme Values. Springer.
return_period.notes: Diverges at the sample maximum; strong nonlinearity. Rank transform of the output followed by monotone transform. Nonlocal (uses empirical ranks).

affine_a2_b1.name: Affine (a=2, b=1) — commutative reference
affine_a2_b1.physical_motivation: Unit conversion (e.g. metres to feet: a=3.281, b=0) or baseline shift. The canonical commutative transform in sensitivity analysis.
affine_a2_b1.canonical_use_case: Negative control: affine transforms preserve all Sobol indices exactly (Proposition 1 in Hettinger 2026).
affine_a2_b1.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer. Wiley.
affine_a2_b1.notes: THE commutative reference: S_i(aY+b) = S_i(Y) for all i, all models. D=0 and Delta=0 by construction. Use as negative control in all experiments.

affine_a05_bm3.name: Affine (a=0.5, b=−3) — unit conversion
affine_a05_bm3.physical_motivation: Negative scale (reflection and compression) plus offset. Models converting from one unit system to another with sign reversal.
affine_a05_bm3.canonical_use_case: Second affine negative control; also tests that negative scale factor does not change Sobol indices.
affine_a05_bm3.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer. Wiley.
affine_a05_bm3.notes: a=0.5<1 compresses; b=-3 shifts. Both preserve relative variance structure. D=0, Delta=0.

tanh_a03.name: Tanh saturation (α=0.3)
tanh_a03.physical_motivation: Amari (1977) neural field activation: local firing rate as bounded function of synaptic potential. Also receptor saturation in dose-response.
tanh_a03.canonical_use_case: Canonical pointwise nonlinear transform for testing non-commutativity; moderate alpha gives large but not extreme index shifts.
tanh_a03.reference_full: Amari, S. I. (1977). Dynamics of pattern formation in lateral-inhibition type neural fields. Biol. Cybern., 27(2), 77–87.
tanh_a03.notes: Concave for y>0, convex for y<0. Moderate saturation at alpha=0.3. Sobol indices shift toward inputs driving the midrange.

tanh_a10.name: Tanh saturation (α=1.0)
tanh_a10.physical_motivation: Standard hyperbolic tangent; stronger saturation than alpha=0.3. Models hard-limiting amplifiers and binary switching.
tanh_a10.canonical_use_case: Strong saturation creates maximum index redistribution for typical Campbell/Ishigami output ranges.
tanh_a10.reference_full: Amari, S. I. (1977). Dynamics of pattern formation in lateral-inhibition type neural fields. Biol. Cybern., 27(2), 77–87.
tanh_a10.notes: Stronger version of tanh_a03. Faster approach to ±1 saturation → heavier sensitivity redistribution.

tanh_a005.name: Tanh saturation (α=0.05, near-linear)
tanh_a005.physical_motivation: Very gentle saturation; nearly affine for typical output ranges. Provides a near-commutative reference for comparison.
tanh_a005.canonical_use_case: Negative control showing that near-affine transforms produce small index changes.
tanh_a005.reference_full: Amari, S. I. (1977). Dynamics of pattern formation in lateral-inhibition type neural fields. Biol. Cybern., 27(2), 77–87.
tanh_a005.notes: At alpha=0.05, tanh(0.05y) ≈ 0.05y for |y|<10 (within 1% of linear). Expected D≈0, Delta≈0.

softplus_b01.name: Softplus (β=0.1, smooth ReLU)
softplus_b01.physical_motivation: Smooth approximation to ReLU used in neural network models and dose-response. Monotone, C-inf, approaches identity for large y.
softplus_b01.canonical_use_case: Softplus activation in neural surrogate models; near-linear for small beta means near-affine behaviour.
softplus_b01.reference_full: Glorot, X., Bordes, A., & Bengio, Y. (2011). Deep sparse rectifier neural networks. AISTATS.
softplus_b01.notes: beta=0.1 → near-linear; asymptotically linear for y>>0. Expected small index changes.

params_description.beta: Softplus sharpness; larger beta approaches ReLU.

softplus_b10.name: Softplus (β=1.0)
softplus_b10.physical_motivation: Standard softplus (beta=1): log(1+exp(y)). Widely used as smooth ReLU approximation in deep learning.
softplus_b10.canonical_use_case: Sensitivity analysis of neural network surrogate model outputs.
softplus_b10.reference_full: Glorot, X., Bordes, A., & Bengio, Y. (2011). Deep sparse rectifier neural networks. AISTATS.
softplus_b10.notes: Concave-convex inflection; 0 for very negative y, linear for very positive y. Medium index shift.

carnot_quadratic.name: Carnot quadratic efficiency
carnot_quadratic.physical_motivation: Models thermodynamic efficiency as a quadratic function of normalised temperature ratio; arises in Carnot engine analysis.
carnot_quadratic.canonical_use_case: Converts temperature field to efficiency field in thermodynamic SA studies.
carnot_quadratic.reference_full: Williams, P. D. (2005). Modelling climate change. Philos. Trans. R. Soc. A.
carnot_quadratic.notes: Quadratic amplification after min-normalisation. Nonlocal. High sensitivity shift toward inputs controlling high-temperature region.

params_description.delta: Regularisation offset to avoid division by zero.

sigmoid_dose.name: Hill dose-response (n=4)
sigmoid_dose.physical_motivation: Hill (1910) dose-response equation: describes the saturation of a receptor/enzyme by a ligand as a function of normalised concentration.
sigmoid_dose.canonical_use_case: Maps concentration-field output to occupancy (0-1) in pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic SA.
sigmoid_dose.reference_full: Hill, A. V. (1910). The possible effects of the aggregation of the molecules of haemoglobin on its dissociation curves. J. Physiol., 40(Suppl), iv–vii.
sigmoid_dose.notes: Hill coefficient n=4 produces steep sigmoid with inflection at EC50. S-shaped, bounded. Nonlocal via min/range normalisation.

params_description.EC50_q: EC50 expressed as quantile of normalised Y.

arrhenius.name: Arrhenius reaction rate
arrhenius.physical_motivation: Arrhenius equation: converts a temperature-like field to reaction rate. The exponential of inverse temperature creates extreme sensitivity in the high-temperature regime.
arrhenius.canonical_use_case: Used in atmospheric chemistry and geothermal SA to map temperature uncertainty to rate-coefficient uncertainty.
arrhenius.reference_full: Saltelli, A., & Tarantola, S. (2002). On the relative importance of input factors. JASA, 97(459), 702–709.
arrhenius.notes: Strongly nonlinear; exp(-Ea/T) amplifies sensitivity at high T. Considered a worst-case for noncommutativity in the original paper.

normalised_stress.name: Goodman normalised stress
normalised_stress.physical_motivation: Normalises output stress by yield stress for Goodman mean-stress correction in fatigue analysis.
normalised_stress.canonical_use_case: Converts structural response field to normalised utilisation ratio in reliability SA.
normalised_stress.reference_full: Socie, D. F., & Marquis, G. B. (2000). Multiaxial Fatigue. SAE International.
normalised_stress.notes: Clips above yield quantile; nonlocal. Creates censored upper tail. High-stress inputs' indices amplified.

weibull_reliability.name: Weibull failure probability
weibull_reliability.physical_motivation: Weibull CDF: probability of failure before normalised time t. Standard in structural and mechanical reliability analysis.
weibull_reliability.canonical_use_case: Converts a normalised load or time field to failure probability in reliability-based SA.
weibull_reliability.reference_full: Weibull, W. (1951). A statistical distribution function of wide applicability. J. Appl. Mech., 18(3), 293–297.
weibull_reliability.notes: Shape k=2 (Rayleigh): concave then convex with inflection. Strongly nonlocal. Good test of CDF-based transforms.

params_description.shape: Weibull shape parameter k; k=1 exponential, k=2 Rayleigh.

johnson_su.name: Johnson SU (arcsinh normalisation)
johnson_su.physical_motivation: Johnson SU family normalisation via arcsinh; handles unbounded data with heavy tails. Used in insurance and engineering reliability.
johnson_su.canonical_use_case: Normalises heavy-tailed engineering outputs for Gaussian-assumption-based analysis.
johnson_su.reference_full: Johnson, N. L. (1949). Systems of frequency curves generated by methods of translation. Biometrika, 36(1/2), 149–176.
johnson_su.notes: arcsinh is monotone, smooth, odd-symmetric, and unbounded. Per-sample centering makes it nonlocal. Near-log for large |y|.

fatigue_miner.name: Palmgren-Miner fatigue accumulation
fatigue_miner.physical_motivation: Palmgren (1924)-Miner (1945) fatigue damage accumulation: D = (stress/stress_ref)^m where m=3 for steel per Paris law.
fatigue_miner.canonical_use_case: Converts a stress-amplitude field to cumulative fatigue damage in structural fatigue SA.
fatigue_miner.reference_full: Miner, M. A. (1945). Cumulative damage in fatigue. J. Appl. Mech., 12(3), A159–A164.
fatigue_miner.notes: Power law m=3 strongly amplifies high stresses. Sensitivity shifted toward inputs controlling peak stress.

params_description.m: Paris-law exponent; m=3 for structural steel, m=4-5 for aluminium.

rankine_failure.name: Rankine failure criterion (max principal)
rankine_failure.physical_motivation: Rankine (maximum normal stress) failure criterion: only tensile stresses matter; compressive stresses set to zero.
rankine_failure.canonical_use_case: Converts a full stress field to active failure-driving stress in structural reliability SA.
rankine_failure.reference_full: Timoshenko, S. P. (1951). Theory of Elasticity. McGraw-Hill.
rankine_failure.notes: ReLU applied to stress field. Non-smooth at zero. Zeros out negative half → sensitivity concentrated in positive tail.

regional_mean.name: Regional mean
regional_mean.physical_motivation: Aggregates the entire spatial field to its mean and broadcasts back. Change-of-support to single global average.
regional_mean.canonical_use_case: Models the coarsest possible spatial aggregation; baseline for COS sensitivity analysis.
regional_mean.reference_full: Gotway, C. A., & Young, L. J. (2002). Combining incompatible spatial data. JASA, 97(458), 632–648.
regional_mean.notes: Destroys all spatial structure; reduces sensitivity analysis to scalar case. Sobol indices reflect global average behaviour.

block_2x2.name: Block average 2×2 (COS)
block_2x2.physical_motivation: Change-of-support at 2x2 block scale. Models spatial aggregation from fine-scale simulation to coarse-scale observations.
block_2x2.canonical_use_case: Spatial SA on block-averaged output to quantify how aggregation changes dominant inputs.
block_2x2.reference_full: Cressie, N. (1993). Statistics for Spatial Data. Wiley.
block_2x2.notes: Linear spatial average operator (preserves Sobol indices only if sensitivity is spatially homogeneous; Proposition 2). Larger block = more aggregation = more commutativity departure.

block_4x4.name: Block average 4×4 (COS)
block_4x4.physical_motivation: Change-of-support at 4x4 block scale. Models spatial aggregation from fine-scale simulation to coarse-scale observations.
block_4x4.canonical_use_case: Spatial SA on block-averaged output to quantify how aggregation changes dominant inputs.
block_4x4.reference_full: Cressie, N. (1993). Statistics for Spatial Data. Wiley.
block_4x4.notes: Linear spatial average operator (preserves Sobol indices only if sensitivity is spatially homogeneous; Proposition 2). Larger block = more aggregation = more commutativity departure.

block_8x8.name: Block average 8×8 (COS)
block_8x8.physical_motivation: Change-of-support at 8x8 block scale. Models spatial aggregation from fine-scale simulation to coarse-scale observations.
block_8x8.canonical_use_case: Spatial SA on block-averaged output to quantify how aggregation changes dominant inputs.
block_8x8.reference_full: Cressie, N. (1993). Statistics for Spatial Data. Wiley.
block_8x8.notes: Linear spatial average operator (preserves Sobol indices only if sensitivity is spatially homogeneous; Proposition 2). Larger block = more aggregation = more commutativity departure.

exceedance_area.name: Exceedance area fraction (q=0.75)
exceedance_area.physical_motivation: Fraction of spatial domain exceeding the 75th percentile; analogous to FEMA flood zone area ratio.
exceedance_area.canonical_use_case: Identifies which inputs control the extent of spatially extreme events in flood and climate SA.
exceedance_area.reference_full: Beven, K., & Binley, A. (1992). The future of distributed models. Hydrological Processes, 6(3), 279–298.
exceedance_area.notes: Scalar nonlinear spatial summary. Discontinuous threshold. Sobol indices on area fraction measure spatial extent sensitivity.

matern_smooth.name: Matérn-1.5 spatial smoothing
matern_smooth.physical_motivation: Matérn ν=1.5 covariance kernel smoothing; standard in geostatistics for once-differentiable spatial fields.
matern_smooth.canonical_use_case: Spatial SA after kriging/smoothing step to compare sensitivity of raw vs. smoothed outputs.
matern_smooth.reference_full: Stein, M. L. (1999). Interpolation of Spatial Data. Springer.
matern_smooth.notes: Linear convolution operator; preserves Sobol indices under spatial homogeneity (Proposition 2). Short length-scale approximates identity.

laplacian_roughness.name: Laplacian roughness |∇²Y|
laplacian_roughness.physical_motivation: Discrete Laplacian magnitude measures spatial roughness; used in image processing and terrain analysis.
laplacian_roughness.canonical_use_case: SA on field roughness to identify which inputs drive spatial heterogeneity rather than mean level.
laplacian_roughness.reference_full: Cressie, N. (1993). Statistics for Spatial Data. Wiley.
laplacian_roughness.notes: Nonlinear (absolute value of second derivative); zero for constant fields. Amplifies sensitivity of spatially heterogeneous inputs.

gradient_magnitude.name: Gradient magnitude |∇Y|
gradient_magnitude.physical_motivation: Gradient magnitude measures spatial rate of change; used in edge detection and topographic slope analysis.
gradient_magnitude.canonical_use_case: SA on spatial gradient fields to identify inputs driving local variability vs. global level.
gradient_magnitude.reference_full: Cressie, N. (1993). Statistics for Spatial Data. Wiley.
gradient_magnitude.notes: Absolute gradient is non-smooth at extrema and minima. Zero for spatially constant fields.

contour_exceedance.name: Signed contour distance (q=0.75)
contour_exceedance.physical_motivation: Signed deviation from exceedance contour; used in flood zone delineation and seismic hazard mapping.
contour_exceedance.canonical_use_case: SA on signed distance to design contour to identify which inputs control exceedance margins.
contour_exceedance.reference_full: Openshaw, S. (1984). The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem. Geobooks.
contour_exceedance.notes: Clipped linear function of normalised deviation. Near-affine in the bulk; clipping creates non-smoothness at ±1.

isoline_length.name: Isoline perimeter (q=0.75)
isoline_length.formula: \phi(Y) = \text{normalised number of threshold crossings on spatial grid}
isoline_length.physical_motivation: Perimeter/length of the spatial exceedance isoline; Euler-characteristic proxy used in spatial statistics.
isoline_length.canonical_use_case: SA on flood or wildfire perimeter extent to identify inputs controlling spatial complexity of events.
isoline_length.reference_full: Openshaw, S. (1984). The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem. Geobooks.
isoline_length.notes: Counts spatial boundary crossings; highly nonlinear (sensitive to threshold location). Broadcast to field shape.

temporal_peak.name: Temporal peak (max_t)
temporal_peak.physical_motivation: Peak response reduction is the most common engineering summary statistic: peak demand, maximum flood stage, PGA.
temporal_peak.canonical_use_case: Temporal SA: identifies inputs driving peak response vs. time-averaged response.
temporal_peak.reference_full: Sudret, B. (2008). Global sensitivity analysis using polynomial chaos expansions. RESS, 93(7), 964–979.
temporal_peak.notes: Nonlinear max reduction. Destroys temporal dynamics; indices may differ greatly from time-average.

temporal_cumsum.name: Cumulative sum (linear)
temporal_cumsum.physical_motivation: Cumulative runoff volume, cumulative drug exposure (AUC), fatigue damage accumulation.
temporal_cumsum.canonical_use_case: Linear temporal operator: negative control confirming commutativity theorem.
temporal_cumsum.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2005). Sensitivity analysis practice. Wiley.
temporal_cumsum.notes: Linear operator: preserves Sobol indices exactly (D=0, Delta=0). Perfect negative control.

temporal_log_cumsum.name: Log cumulative sum
temporal_log_cumsum.physical_motivation: Log-transformed cumulative exposure; AUC in pharmacokinetics reported on log scale.
temporal_log_cumsum.canonical_use_case: Log-AUC sensitivity analysis to identify inputs driving integrated drug exposure.
temporal_log_cumsum.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2005). Sensitivity analysis practice. Wiley.
temporal_log_cumsum.notes: Nonlinear composition: log∘cumsum. Concave. Some index shift expected.

params_description.eps: Small positive shift to ensure log-argument is positive.

temporal_exceedance_duration.name: Exceedance duration (q=0.75)
temporal_exceedance_duration.physical_motivation: Duration above threshold: flood duration, heat-wave length, pharmacological drug action time.
temporal_exceedance_duration.canonical_use_case: SA on exceedance duration to identify inputs governing how long a system exceeds a critical level.
temporal_exceedance_duration.reference_full: Coles, S. (2001). An Introduction to Statistical Modeling of Extreme Values. Springer.
temporal_exceedance_duration.notes: Nonlinear: counts threshold crossings up to time t. Non-smooth at crossings.

temporal_envelope.name: Running maximum envelope
temporal_envelope.physical_motivation: Running maximum in structural dynamics (maximum response envelope), extreme value characterisation.
temporal_envelope.canonical_use_case: SA on response envelope to identify inputs driving worst-case time evolution.
temporal_envelope.reference_full: Sudret, B. (2008). Global sensitivity analysis using polynomial chaos expansions. RESS, 93(7), 964–979.
temporal_envelope.notes: Non-decreasing monotone operator; highly nonlinear in sensitivity sense. Amplifies sensitivity of late-time inputs.

temporal_bandpass.name: Bandpass filter (linear)
temporal_bandpass.formula: \phi(Y)_t = \sum_k \hat{Y}_k e^{2\pi i k t/T} \cdot \mathbf{1}[f_{low} \leq k \leq f_{high}]
temporal_bandpass.physical_motivation: Zero-phase FFT bandpass: isolates a frequency band. Used in seismology, climatology, and signal conditioning.
temporal_bandpass.canonical_use_case: Linear temporal operator: negative control alongside cumulative sum.
temporal_bandpass.reference_full: Rabiner, L. R., & Gold, B. (1975). Theory and Application of Digital Signal Processing. Prentice-Hall.
temporal_bandpass.notes: Linear convolution in frequency domain; preserves Sobol indices (D=0, Delta=0). Selects frequencies [0.05, 0.30] × Nyquist.

temporal_block_avg.name: Temporal block avg (temporal COS)
temporal_block_avg.physical_motivation: Temporal aggregation: daily→monthly→annual. Coarsens time resolution analogously to spatial COS.
temporal_block_avg.canonical_use_case: SA on temporally aggregated output vs. fine-scale output to study temporal change-of-support.
temporal_block_avg.reference_full: Gotway, C. A., & Young, L. J. (2002). Combining incompatible spatial data. JASA.
temporal_block_avg.notes: Linear averaging operator; Sobol indices preserved when sensitivity is temporally homogeneous (Proposition 2 temporal analogue).

params_description.block: Number of time steps to average per block.

temporal_rms.name: Temporal RMS
temporal_rms.physical_motivation: Root mean square energy of a time series: fundamental in structural vibration and acoustics.
temporal_rms.canonical_use_case: SA on RMS response to identify inputs controlling average signal power.
temporal_rms.notes: Nonlinear (sqrt of quadratic mean). Even function inside integral → sensitive to magnitude, not sign.

temporal_range.name: Temporal range max−min
temporal_range.physical_motivation: Peak-to-peak amplitude: measure of dynamic range used in structural engineering and climate variability.
temporal_range.canonical_use_case: SA on signal amplitude to identify inputs governing dynamic range rather than mean level.
temporal_range.notes: Nonlinear (difference of max and min); scalar broadcast. Sensitive to both tails simultaneously.

temporal_autocorr.name: Lag-1 temporal autocorrelation
temporal_autocorr.physical_motivation: Lag-1 autocorrelation measures short-term temporal persistence; important in climate and hydrological time series.
temporal_autocorr.canonical_use_case: SA on autocorrelation structure to identify which inputs control temporal smoothness of the model output.
temporal_autocorr.reference_full: Box, G. E. P., Jenkins, G. M., Reinsel, G. C., & Ljung, G. M. (2015). Time Series Analysis. Wiley.
temporal_autocorr.notes: Bounded [-1,1]; highly nonlinear function of covariance structure. Expected large index shifts.

temporal_quantile_q50.name: Temporal median (q=0.50)
temporal_quantile_q50.physical_motivation: Temporal median: robust central tendency used when time series contains outliers.
temporal_quantile_q50.canonical_use_case: Robust SA summary that is less sensitive to temporal extremes than the mean.
temporal_quantile_q50.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer.
temporal_quantile_q50.notes: Nonlinear (nonsmooth at discontinuities); near-linear for well-behaved distributions. Nonlocal.

temporal_quantile_q10.name: Temporal lower decile (q=0.10)
temporal_quantile_q10.physical_motivation: Lower decile: drought-flow metric (Q10 low-flow), minimum-return analysis.
temporal_quantile_q10.canonical_use_case: SA on lower tail behaviour to identify which inputs control dry/low conditions.
temporal_quantile_q10.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer.
temporal_quantile_q10.notes: Lower quantile more sensitive to low-valued inputs than mean or median.

temporal_quantile_q90.name: Temporal upper decile (q=0.90)
temporal_quantile_q90.physical_motivation: Upper decile: flood-flow metric (Q90 high-flow), heat-stress analysis.
temporal_quantile_q90.canonical_use_case: SA on upper tail to identify drivers of high-flow / high-temperature extremes.
temporal_quantile_q90.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer.
temporal_quantile_q90.notes: Upper quantile amplifies high-value input sensitivity.

rank_transform.name: Rank / PIT transform
rank_transform.analytical_sobol_tractable: Rank/PIT transform converts any continuous distribution to uniform. Used in nonparametric SA (SRRC) and copula modelling.
rank_transform.physical_motivation: Standard preprocessing in nonparametric SA: converts model outputs to ranks for rank-correlation sensitivity measures.
rank_transform.reference_dois: Iman, R. L., & Conover, W. J. (1979). The use of the rank transform in regression. Technometrics, 21(4), 499–509.
rank_transform.reference_full: Monotone but step-function: not smooth. Destroys relative spacing; preserves only ordinal structure. Sobol indices on ranks measure ordinal sensitivity.

standardised_anomaly.name: Standardised anomaly (Z-score)
standardised_anomaly.physical_motivation: Z-score standardisation: removes mean and scales by standard deviation. Standard preprocessing in statistical modelling.
standardised_anomaly.canonical_use_case: Standardisation before SA to compare sensitivity across models with different units/scales.
standardised_anomaly.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer. Wiley.
standardised_anomaly.notes: Affine per-sample: Z = (Y - mu)/sigma. Preserves Sobol indices (D=0, Delta=0) exactly within a sample. Nonlocal across samples.

entropy_proxy.name: Entropy proxy −(Y−mean)²
entropy_proxy.physical_motivation: Negative squared deviation as a proxy for information entropy (maximised at mean). Models entropy-penalised outputs.
entropy_proxy.canonical_use_case: Tests sensitivity analysis with concave, even, non-monotone transformation.
entropy_proxy.reference_full: Shannon, C. E. (1948). A mathematical theory of communication. Bell System Technical Journal, 27(3), 379–423.
entropy_proxy.notes: Concave, even, non-monotone: destroys order. High values map to low outputs and vice versa. Strong index redistribution.

quantile_transform.name: Quantile normalise (empirical CDF)
quantile_transform.analytical_sobol_tractable: Empirical CDF quantile normalisation (probability integral transform). Produces uniform marginals.
quantile_transform.physical_motivation: Nonparametric preprocessing that guarantees uniform marginals regardless of the original distribution.
quantile_transform.reference_dois: Blom, G. (1958). Statistical Estimates and Transformed Beta Variables. Wiley.
quantile_transform.reference_full: Monotone step function; nonlocal (uses ranks across samples). Equivalent to rank transform divided by n+1.

winsorise_q10_q90.name: Winsorise to [q10, q90]
winsorise_q10_q90.analytical_sobol_tractable: Winsorisation: robust truncation of extreme values by capping at specified quantiles.
winsorise_q10_q90.physical_motivation: Outlier-resistant preprocessing in robust SA to reduce influence of extreme model outputs.
winsorise_q10_q90.reference_dois: Tukey, J. W. (1977). Exploratory Data Analysis. Addison-Wesley.
winsorise_q10_q90.reference_full: Piecewise-linear; identity in [Q10, Q90], flat outside. Near-affine in bulk → small index shift.

yeo_johnson.name: Yeo-Johnson transform (λ=0.5)
yeo_johnson.physical_motivation: Extends Box-Cox to all of ℝ; used for normalising residuals and model outputs spanning both positive and negative values.
yeo_johnson.canonical_use_case: SA preprocessing for outputs that can be negative (e.g. anomalies, residuals).
yeo_johnson.reference_full: Yeo, I. K., & Johnson, R. A. (2000). A new family of power transformations to improve normality or symmetry. Biometrika, 87(4), 954–959.
yeo_johnson.notes: C1 at y=0 (piecewise smooth); monotone; concave for y>0, convex for y<0 at lambda=0.5.

params_description.lam: Power exponent; lambda=1 gives near-identity, lambda=0 gives log/arcsinh.

inverse_normal.name: Inverse normal score (probit)
inverse_normal.physical_motivation: Inverse normal (Blom) scores: transforms any distribution to standard normal. Used in genome-wide SA and normalisation.
inverse_normal.canonical_use_case: Guarantees normality of transformed output for assumption-based SA methods.
inverse_normal.reference_full: Blom, G. (1958). Statistical Estimates and Transformed Beta Variables. Wiley.
inverse_normal.notes: Monotone; expands central quantiles, stretches tails to ±∞. Nonlocal. Stronger index shift than rank transform.

square_pointwise.name: Square (convex, even)
square_pointwise.physical_motivation: Variance-of-mean decomposition in higher-order SA; quadratic response surfaces; L2 regularisation.
square_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Tests noncommutativity of even, convex functions; analytically: S_i(Y^2) redistributes weight to inputs with high-variance contribution to E[Y|X_i]^2.
square_pointwise.reference_full: Hardy, G. H., Littlewood, J. E., & Pólya, G. (1934). Inequalities. Cambridge University Press.
square_pointwise.notes: phi''=2>0 everywhere (strictly convex). Even: maps positive and negative values to same output. S1 for input i = Var[E[Y^2|X_i]] / Var[Y^2]; analytically complex.

exp_pointwise.name: Exponential (convex, monotone)
exp_pointwise.physical_motivation: Exponential transfer function in neural networks, log-linear models, and Arrhenius kinetics.
exp_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Canonical strictly convex monotone pointwise transform; by Jensen E[e^Y]>e^{E[Y]}, amplifying high-value sensitivity.
exp_pointwise.reference_full: Hardy, G. H., Littlewood, J. E., & Pólya, G. (1934). Inequalities. Cambridge.
exp_pointwise.notes: phi''=alpha^2 e^{alpha y}>0. For Y~N(mu,sigma): Var[e^{alpha Y}]=e^{2 alpha mu+alpha^2 sigma^2}(e^{alpha^2 sigma^2}-1).

params_description.scale: Alpha exponent; larger alpha → stronger amplification.

cube_pointwise.name: Cube (odd, inflection at 0)
cube_pointwise.physical_motivation: Cubic nonlinearity in mechanical systems (Duffing oscillator); cubic spline basis function.
cube_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Odd convex/concave: tests asymmetric index redistribution for odd functions.
cube_pointwise.reference_full: Borwein, J. M., & Lewis, A. S. (2000). Convex Analysis and Nonlinear Optimization. Springer.
cube_pointwise.notes: phi'''=6!=0: third derivative nonzero everywhere (odd polynomial). phi''=6y changes sign at 0.

cosh_pointwise.name: Cosh (convex, even, C∞)
cosh_pointwise.physical_motivation: Symmetric exponential growth; models symmetric bistable potentials in statistical mechanics.
cosh_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Even convex function: maps both tails to high values; tests symmetric sensitivity amplification.
cosh_pointwise.reference_full: Abramowitz, M., & Stegun, I. A. (1972). Handbook of Mathematical Functions. Dover.
cosh_pointwise.notes: phi''=alpha^2 cosh(alpha y)>0 everywhere. Even: symmetry around y=0. Minimum at y=0.

params_description.scale: Alpha controls width; larger alpha -> steeper growth.

relu_pointwise.name: ReLU max(y,0) (convex, non-smooth)
relu_pointwise.physical_motivation: Rectified linear unit: standard deep learning activation; eliminates negative activations.
relu_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Tests noncommutativity of non-smooth convex monotone transform. Zeros out negative output half.
relu_pointwise.reference_full: Glorot, X., Bordes, A., & Bengio, Y. (2011). Deep sparse rectifier NNs. AISTATS.
relu_pointwise.notes: Kink at y=0 (C0 only). Sensitivity for inputs driving negative Y is zeroed out. High expected index shift.

softmax_shift.name: Shifted softmax normalisation
softmax_shift.physical_motivation: Softmax normalisation in classification models; converts logits to probability simplex.
softmax_shift.canonical_use_case: SA on softmax-normalised outputs to identify sensitivity on the probability simplex.
softmax_shift.reference_full: Goodfellow, I., Bengio, Y., & Courville, A. (2016). Deep Learning. MIT Press.
softmax_shift.notes: Nonlocal (uses sum across all outputs). Bounded in (0,1). Invariant to constant shift.

log1p_positive.name: log(1+|y|) (signed)
log1p_positive.physical_motivation: Signed log-modulus: handles data spanning negative and positive values with heavy-tailed distribution.
log1p_positive.canonical_use_case: SA preprocessing for outputs with both positive and negative values and heavy tails.
log1p_positive.reference_full: John, J. A., & Draper, N. R. (1980). An alternative family of transformations. J. Roy. Stat. Soc. C, 29(2), 190–197.
log1p_positive.notes: C1 at y=0 (derivative = ±1). Concave for y>0: compresses positive tail. Odd symmetry.

sqrt_abs.name: sqrt(|y|) signed
sqrt_abs.physical_motivation: Square-root magnitude transform; variance-stabilising for Poisson-like non-negative data.
sqrt_abs.canonical_use_case: Tests sensitivity under concave square-root with kink at zero.
sqrt_abs.reference_full: Anscombe, F. J. (1948). The transformation of Poisson, binomial and negative-binomial data. Biometrika, 35(3-4), 246-254.
sqrt_abs.notes: C0 at y=0 (derivative → ±∞). phi''=-1/(4|y|^{3/2}) for y>0 (concave). Medium index shift.

cbrt_pointwise.name: Cube root (monotone, concave y>0)
cbrt_pointwise.physical_motivation: Cube root is the standard variance-stabilising transform for chi-squared-distributed quantities and counts.
cbrt_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Tests monotone concave transform with singularity in second derivative at origin.
cbrt_pointwise.notes: phi = y^{1/3}: derivative = (1/3)y^{-2/3}→∞ as y→0. Concave for y>0, convex for y<0.

neg_square.name: Negative square -y² (concave, even)
neg_square.physical_motivation: Strictly concave downward parabola. Models diminishing returns, entropy maximisation, and negative feedback.
neg_square.canonical_use_case: Even concave transform: tests dual of square_pointwise.
neg_square.reference_full: Hardy, G. H., et al. (1934). Inequalities. Cambridge.
neg_square.notes: phi''=-2<0 (strictly concave). Maps large |y| to large negative. Non-monotone.

logistic_pointwise.name: Logistic σ(y) (monotone, S-shaped)
logistic_pointwise.physical_motivation: Logistic function: standard sigmoid for probability modelling, logistic regression output layer.
logistic_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Tests S-shaped monotone transform; inflection at y=0 separates convex/concave behaviour.
logistic_pointwise.reference_full: Verhulst, P.-F. (1838). Notice sur la loi que la population suit dans son accroissement. Corresp. Math. Phys., 10, 113–121.
logistic_pointwise.notes: phi''= k^2 sigma(y)(1-sigma(y))(1-2sigma(y)): sign changes at inflection. Concave for y>0, convex for y<0.

params_description.k: Logistic slope parameter; larger k gives steeper sigmoid.

arctan_pointwise.name: arctan(y) (monotone, bounded)
arctan_pointwise.physical_motivation: Arctan bounded saturation; used in circuit analysis (transistor operating point), fuzzy logic, and probability theory.
arctan_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Smooth bounded monotone S-shaped transform; analytically tractable via arctan integral.
arctan_pointwise.reference_full: Abramowitz, M., & Stegun, I. A. (1972). Handbook of Mathematical Functions. Dover.
arctan_pointwise.notes: phi''= -2 alpha^3 y / (1 + alpha^2 y^2)^2: concave for y>0, convex for y<0. Odd.

params_description.scale: Alpha scaling; larger = faster approach to bounds.

erf_pointwise.name: erf(y) (monotone, S-shaped, C∞)
erf_pointwise.physical_motivation: Error function: related to Gaussian CDF; arises in diffusion problems, Gaussian random variable probabilities.
erf_pointwise.canonical_use_case: SA on probability of exceeding a Gaussian threshold; erf(Y) maps output to exceedance probability.
erf_pointwise.reference_full: Abramowitz, M., & Stegun, I. A. (1972). Handbook of Mathematical Functions. Dover.
erf_pointwise.notes: Smooth, monotone, odd. phi''=-4alpha^3 y/sqrt(pi) exp(-alpha^2 y^2): concave for y>0.

sinh_pointwise.name: sinh(y) (monotone, convex y>0)
sinh_pointwise.physical_motivation: Hyperbolic sine: grows exponentially; models catenary cables, hanging chain problems, and hyperbolic geometry.
sinh_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Odd monotone transform that expands both tails; complementary to tanh (bounded).
sinh_pointwise.reference_full: Abramowitz, M., & Stegun, I. A. (1972). Handbook of Mathematical Functions. Dover.
sinh_pointwise.notes: phi''=alpha^2 sinh(alpha y): convex for y>0, concave for y<0. Unbounded.

sin_pointwise.name: sin(y) (periodic, C∞)
sin_pointwise.physical_motivation: Sine activation; models periodic outputs (ocean tides, seasonal cycles), Fourier series basis.
sin_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Tests periodic non-monotone transform; local sensitivity reversals expected.
sin_pointwise.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2010). Variance based sensitivity analysis of model output. CPC, 181(2), 259–270.
sin_pointwise.notes: Infinitely many sign changes in phi'. phi''=-f^2 sin(fy) oscillates. Expected high index redistribution for oscillatory outputs.

cos_pointwise.name: cos(y) (periodic, even, C∞)
cos_pointwise.physical_motivation: Cosine activation; even function, models phase-shifted oscillatory systems.
cos_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Even periodic transform; tests how even symmetry interacts with asymmetric sensitivity structure.
cos_pointwise.notes: phi''=-f^2 cos(fy): alternates sign. Even: maps ±y to same output. High redistribution.

abs_pointwise.name: |y| (convex, non-smooth at 0)
abs_pointwise.physical_motivation: Absolute value: L1-norm component; used in LASSO, robust statistics, and total variation regularisation.
abs_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Non-smooth convex even function; tests sensitivity under kink at origin.
abs_pointwise.reference_full: Hardy, G. H., et al. (1934). Inequalities. Cambridge.
abs_pointwise.notes: phi'= sign(y) (undefined at 0). Even: maps ±y to same output. Destroys sign information.

step_pointwise.name: Heaviside step (discontinuous)
step_pointwise.physical_motivation: Heaviside threshold: Boolean exceedance. Used in Monte Carlo failure analysis (performance function).
step_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Maximum possible noncommutativity: reduces everything to binary. Sobol indices measure probability-of-exceedance sensitivity.
step_pointwise.notes: Completely discontinuous. Destroys all quantitative information. D→max, Delta→max.

triangle_wave.name: Triangle wave (periodic, non-smooth)
triangle_wave.physical_motivation: Triangle wave: piecewise linear periodic function; basic waveform in signal generators.
triangle_wave.canonical_use_case: Tests C0 periodic non-smooth transform: expected medium-to-high index redistribution.
triangle_wave.reference_full: Stein, E. M., & Shakarchi, R. (2003). Fourier Analysis. Princeton University Press.
triangle_wave.notes: Piecewise linear, continuous but not differentiable at peaks. Bounded. Period T.

smooth_bump.name: Smooth bump (C∞ compact support)
smooth_bump.physical_motivation: Mollifier (bump function): C∞ function with compact support; used in PDE smoothing and wavelet construction.
smooth_bump.canonical_use_case: Tests C∞ compact-support transform with sharp cutoff at ±width; interesting sensitivity structure near support boundary.
smooth_bump.reference_full: Wendland, H. (2004). Scattered Data Approximation. Cambridge University Press.
smooth_bump.notes: Zero outside ±width, smooth inside. All derivatives exist and vanish at boundary. Infinitely flat near edge.

params_description.width: Half-width of support; values outside [-width, width] map to 0.

rational_quadratic.name: Rational quadratic 1/(1+y²)
rational_quadratic.physical_motivation: Cauchy kernel / Lorentzian profile: models resonance peaks in spectroscopy, Cauchy distribution density.
rational_quadratic.canonical_use_case: Tests rational function transform; related to Cauchy distribution and spectral analysis.
rational_quadratic.reference_full: Rasmussen, C. E., & Williams, C. K. I. (2006). Gaussian Processes for Machine Learning. MIT Press.
rational_quadratic.notes: phi''=2(3y^2-1)/(1+y^2)^3: convex for |y|<1/sqrt(3), concave outside. Symmetric.

inverse_pointwise.name: 1/(|y|+ε) (convex, singular at 0)
inverse_pointwise.physical_motivation: Inverse magnitude: maps small values to large and vice versa. Models inverse-square force laws, optical depth.
inverse_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Tests rank-reversing transform; inputs that drive small Y become influential after inversion.
inverse_pointwise.reference_full: Hardy, G. H., et al. (1934). Inequalities. Cambridge.
inverse_pointwise.notes: Monotone decreasing in |y|: reverses sensitivity ranking. High epsilon → near-constant.

log_abs_pointwise.name: log(|y|+ε) (signed)
log_abs_pointwise.physical_motivation: Log absolute value: compresses both tails symmetrically; used in log-odds models and hyperbolic geometry.
log_abs_pointwise.canonical_use_case: Tests even concave (for |y|>0) log transform symmetric about origin.
log_abs_pointwise.reference_full: Box, G. E. P., & Cox, D. R. (1964). JRSS-B.
log_abs_pointwise.notes: Even, unbounded below. phi'' = -1/(|y|+eps)^2 < 0 (concave for |y|>0). Near-zero values compressed.

min_max_normalise.name: Min-max normalisation [0,1]
min_max_normalise.physical_motivation: Standard [0,1] rescaling; invariant to monotone transformations in rank sense. Used in machine learning normalisation.
min_max_normalise.canonical_use_case: Near-affine normalisation; nearly preserves Sobol indices (nonlocal due to per-sample min/max).
min_max_normalise.reference_full: Saltelli, A., et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis: The Primer.
min_max_normalise.notes: Affine per-sample: S_i preserved within a sample. Nonlocal across samples → small index shift. Expected D≈0.

robust_scale.name: Robust scaling (IQR-based)
robust_scale.physical_motivation: IQR-based robust standardisation; resistant to outliers. Used in outlier-robust preprocessing of simulation outputs.
robust_scale.canonical_use_case: Robust near-affine preprocessing: small expected index shift, robust to extreme samples.
robust_scale.reference_full: Huber, P. J. (1981). Robust Statistics. Wiley.
robust_scale.notes: Affine after centering on median: near-affine. Small D, Delta expected.

clamp_sigma.name: Clamp to ±2σ (soft Winsorisation)
clamp_sigma.physical_motivation: Sigma-clipping: standard in astronomy and signal processing to remove outliers beyond 2 standard deviations.
clamp_sigma.canonical_use_case: Soft Winsorisation; tests impact of outlier removal on Sobol indices.
clamp_sigma.reference_full: Tukey, J. W. (1977). Exploratory Data Analysis. Addison-Wesley.
clamp_sigma.notes: Piecewise linear. Near-identity in bulk (95% of normal distribution). Small expected index change.

params_description.n_sigma: Number of standard deviations for clipping bounds.

algebraic_sigmoid.name: Algebraic sigmoid y/sqrt(1+y^2)
algebraic_sigmoid.physical_motivation: Algebraic sigmoid; computationally cheap bounded activation.
algebraic_sigmoid.notes: Similar to tanh but heavier tails. Concave for y>0.

anomaly_pct.name: Anomaly percent departure from mean
anomaly_pct.physical_motivation: Percentage anomaly from mean; standard in climate science for departure indices.
anomaly_pct.canonical_use_case: Climate model SA; percentage departure analysis.
anomaly_pct.notes: Nonlocal (uses sample mean). Large anomalies amplified; mean shifts to 0. Near-affine for small departures.

anscombe.name: Anscombe VST (Poisson counts)
anscombe.physical_motivation: Variance-stabilising transform for Poisson counts; standard in image processing and astronomy.
anscombe.canonical_use_case: Count data SA; Poisson-like output analysis.
anscombe.notes: Approximately makes Var~1 for Poisson data. Square-root family: concave, compresses high counts.

asinh_vst.name: Inverse hyperbolic sine (VST, C-inf)
asinh_vst.physical_motivation: Inverse hyperbolic sine (IHS) transform; VST for data with zeros and negatives.
asinh_vst.canonical_use_case: Finance (negative returns), ecology; works for all reals including 0.
asinh_vst.notes: Like log for large |y|; linear near 0. Handles negative values unlike log. Concave for y>0.

atan2pi.name: (2/pi)*arctan (bounded monotone)
atan2pi.physical_motivation: Bounded monotone activation; saturation in neural/biological models.
atan2pi.notes: Maps R to (-1,1). Concave for y>0. Smoother saturation than tanh.

bent_identity.name: Bent identity (monotone, near-linear)
bent_identity.physical_motivation: Bent identity; near-linear with slight curvature; smooth alternative to ReLU.
bent_identity.notes: Very close to identity for large |y|. Small SA index changes expected.

bernstein_b3.name: Bernstein B3 basis polynomial
bernstein_b3.physical_motivation: Bernstein basis polynomial used in Bezier curves and polynomial regression.
bernstein_b3.canonical_use_case: Non-monotone hump shape; unimodal SA redistribution.
bernstein_b3.reference_full: Bernstein, S. (1912). Demonstration du theoreme de Weierstrass.
bernstein_b3.notes: Maps [0,1] to [0,4/9] with peak at u=2/3. Non-monotone.

bias_correction.name: Bias correction (linear scaling to 1)
bias_correction.physical_motivation: Linear scaling bias correction; normalises model output to unit mean.
bias_correction.canonical_use_case: Climate model output SA after bias correction.
bias_correction.reference_full: Piani et al. (2010). Theor Appl Climatol 99.
bias_correction.notes: Divides by sample mean. Approximately affine (linear scaling). SA indices near-preserved.

bimodal_flip.name: Bimodal flip (zero crossings at 0,0.5,1)
bimodal_flip.physical_motivation: Bimodal/antisymmetric function mapping [0,1] to itself with zero at 0.5.
bimodal_flip.notes: Zero at u=0, u=0.5, u=1. Negative on (0,0.5), positive on (0.5,1). C-inf polynomial.

breakpoint.name: Piecewise linear breakpoint (C0)
breakpoint.physical_motivation: Piecewise linear with slope change; regime switching; threshold exceedance models.
breakpoint.notes: C0 at breakpoint. Different sensitivities above/below threshold.

chebyshev_t4.name: Chebyshev T4 polynomial
chebyshev_t4.physical_motivation: Chebyshev T4 polynomial; equioscillation property; used in spectral methods.
chebyshev_t4.canonical_use_case: PCE studies; oscillatory non-monotone SA analysis.
chebyshev_t4.notes: Even, 3 local extrema in (-1,1). Strong index reshuffling expected.

chord_normalise.name: Chord normalisation (L2 sphere proj)
chord_normalise.physical_motivation: Chord normalisation; L2 sphere projection; removes total abundance effect.
chord_normalise.canonical_use_case: Species composition SA independent of total abundance.
chord_normalise.notes: Projects each sample onto the unit sphere. Nonlocal (uses L2 norm across outputs). Removes scale.

cos_squared.name: cos^2(fy) (even, non-negative)
cos_squared.notes: Phase-shifted sin_squared by pi/2. Mean=0.5. sin^2+cos^2=1 (complementary).

cumulative_damage.name: Cumulative damage |y|^m m=3 (Miner)
cumulative_damage.physical_motivation: Palmgren-Miner cumulative damage; S-N curve; fatigue life accumulation.
cumulative_damage.canonical_use_case: Fatigue SA; damage accumulation under stress cycles.
cumulative_damage.notes: Power law m=3. Even, convex for y>0. More aggressive than square for large outputs.

cvar_q95.name: CVaR/ES q=0.95 (nonlocal tail mean)
cvar_q95.physical_motivation: Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR/Expected Shortfall): average loss in worst 5%; coherent risk measure.
cvar_q95.notes: Coherent risk measure (subadditive). More sensitive to tail inputs than VaR.

damped_sin.name: Damped sine (decaying oscillation)
damped_sin.physical_motivation: Damped oscillator; vibration signal envelope in structural dynamics.
damped_sin.notes: Odd function, zero at y=0. Amplitude decays as exp(-d|y|). Non-monotone.

deadzone.name: Deadzone (zero band, C0)
deadzone.physical_motivation: Dead zone in control systems; sensor dead-band; yield gap.
deadzone.notes: Zeros out values in (-w,w). Symmetric soft threshold. C0 at +-w.

donut.name: Donut/ring indicator (C-inf)
donut.physical_motivation: Ring-shaped response; annular sensitivity in spatial models; band-pass analogue.
donut.canonical_use_case: Annular sensitivity; response to outputs near a specific magnitude.
donut.notes: Even. Peaks at |y|=radius. C-inf smooth with non-smooth-looking ring structure.

double_sin.name: Double-frequency sin+sin (interference)
double_sin.canonical_use_case: Multi-frequency oscillatory SA study; frequency mixing.
double_sin.notes: Interference pattern depends on frequency ratio. Incommensurable frequencies give quasi-periodic output.

drawdown.name: Max drawdown (temporal, nonlocal)
drawdown.physical_motivation: Maximum drawdown; portfolio loss from peak; key financial risk metric.
drawdown.reference_full: Magdon-Ismail et al. (2004). IEEE Trans NN.
drawdown.notes: Temporal transform (requires 2D input). Running max is piecewise smooth. Output <= 0 always.

dual_power_lam03.name: Dual power lam=0.3 (C1, all-real)
dual_power_lam03.physical_motivation: Yeo-Johnson power transform; handles both positive and negative values; MLE-optimal VST.
dual_power_lam03.canonical_use_case: General purpose normalisation; SA of non-normal outputs.
dual_power_lam03.notes: Piecewise power; C1 at y=0. More flexible than Box-Cox since handles negatives.

emax_model.name: Emax pharmacodynamic model n=1
emax_model.physical_motivation: Emax pharmacodynamic model; maximum drug effect; IC50/EC50 binding.
emax_model.canonical_use_case: PD SA; maximum effect saturation analysis.
emax_model.notes: Bounded [0,Emax]. Monotone. n=1: Michaelis-Menten. Concave above ED50.

energy_distance.name: Energy distance proxy (E-statistic)
energy_distance.physical_motivation: Energy distance (Szekely & Rizzo 2013); statistical distance between distributions.
energy_distance.canonical_use_case: Distribution comparison SA; energy-based divergence sensitivity.
energy_distance.reference_full: Szekely & Rizzo (2013). J Stat Plan Inf 143(8).
energy_distance.notes: E-statistic proxy: mean |u| where u is standardised. Nonlocal.

excess_return.name: Excess return (mean-centred)
excess_return.physical_motivation: Mean-centred output; excess return over benchmark; demeaned signal.
excess_return.notes: Linear in Y (given fixed mean). Nonlocal (uses sample mean). SA indices preserved within sample.

exp_neg_sq.name: Gaussian kernel exp(-s^2*y^2) (C-inf)
exp_neg_sq.physical_motivation: Gaussian kernel; RBF; density of standard normal; squashed-hill shape.
exp_neg_sq.canonical_use_case: SA where outputs near 0 are most sensitive; Gaussian weighting.
exp_neg_sq.reference_full: Rasmussen & Williams (2006). Gaussian Processes for ML. MIT Press.
exp_neg_sq.notes: Concave near 0, convex on tails. Non-monotone. Kills sensitivity to large-output inputs.

exp_pos_sq.name: Anti-Gaussian exp(+s^2*y^2) (convex)
exp_pos_sq.physical_motivation: Anti-Gaussian; extreme tail amplification; super-exponential growth.
exp_pos_sq.notes: Clipped to avoid overflow. Very strongly amplifies large |y|. SA dominated by extreme inputs.

fold_change.name: Log2 fold-change from sample mean
fold_change.physical_motivation: Log2 fold-change from mean; genomics (DESeq2); finance (log-return).
fold_change.notes: Log of relative value vs sample mean. Monotone in Y for fixed mean. Nonlocal.

freeman_tukey.name: Freeman-Tukey VST (count data)
freeman_tukey.physical_motivation: Freeman-Tukey arc-sine VST for Poisson counts; more stable than Anscombe for small counts.
freeman_tukey.notes: More stable than Anscombe for counts near 0. Concave.

gev_cdf.name: GEV CDF xi=0.3 (Frechet type)
gev_cdf.physical_motivation: GEV CDF; extreme value theory; return level analysis.
gev_cdf.reference_full: Jenkinson (1955). Q J R Meteorol Soc 81(348).
gev_cdf.notes: xi>0: Frechet family; heavy right tail. Bounded [0,1]. Nonlinear S-shape.

gompertz_cdf.name: Gompertz CDF (asymmetric sigmoid)
gompertz_cdf.physical_motivation: Gompertz growth model; asymmetric S-curve used in population dynamics and cancer modelling.
gompertz_cdf.reference_full: Gompertz, B. (1825). Phil Trans R Soc.
gompertz_cdf.notes: Asymmetric: slower rise for small u, faster for large. Breaks symmetry of logistic.

growing_degree_days.name: Growing degree days base=10
growing_degree_days.physical_motivation: Growing degree days: accumulated heat above base temperature; agricultural phenology.
growing_degree_days.canonical_use_case: Agricultural SA; crop model sensitivity to temperature threshold.
growing_degree_days.notes: Pointwise ReLU-shift with base temperature. Zero for sub-threshold days. Strong SA redistribution.

hard_sigmoid.name: Hard sigmoid (piecewise linear, C0)
hard_sigmoid.physical_motivation: Computationally cheap sigmoid approximation; piecewise linear bounded activation.
hard_sigmoid.reference_full: Goodfellow et al. (2016). Deep Learning. MIT Press.
hard_sigmoid.notes: C0 at kink points. Nonsmooth. Affine in the active region.

hard_tanh.name: Hard tanh clip(-1,1) (piecewise, C0)
hard_tanh.physical_motivation: Hard clipping; used in quantised neural networks; represents saturation.
hard_tanh.notes: Piecewise linear. C0 at +-1/s. Affine for |sy|<1.

hard_threshold.name: Hard threshold (discontinuous)
hard_threshold.notes: Jump discontinuities at +-lambda. Kills outputs in dead band.

hellinger.name: Hellinger transform (chi^2 equaliser)
hellinger.physical_motivation: Hellinger distance equaliser; standard in community ecology (Legendre & Gallagher 2001).
hellinger.canonical_use_case: Ecological community data SA; double-zero problem avoidance.
hellinger.notes: Square-root of relative abundance. Downweights dominant species. Useful for chi-squared ordination.

hermite_he2.name: Hermite He2 polynomial
hermite_he2.physical_motivation: Probabilist Hermite polynomial He2; basis for Hermite PCE.
hermite_he2.canonical_use_case: PCE; quadratic SA study; even function.
hermite_he2.notes: Quadratic with minimum at 0; loses sign information. Equivalent to neg_square shifted.

hermite_he3.name: Hermite He3 polynomial
hermite_he3.physical_motivation: Probabilist Hermite He3; two extrema, non-monotone.
hermite_he3.notes: Non-monotone for small inputs (extrema at u=+-1). Strongly reshuffles SA indices.

hill_response.name: Hill equation response n=2 (saturation)
hill_response.physical_motivation: Hill equation: sigmoidal dose-response; receptor occupancy; enzyme kinetics.
hill_response.notes: S-shaped CDF on [0,infty). n=2: steeper than logistic. Concave above EC50, convex below. Bounded [0,1].

hockey_stick.name: Hockey stick ReLU-shift (convex, C0)
hockey_stick.physical_motivation: ReLU-shift; option payoff function; damage exceedance.
hockey_stick.notes: Kills all sensitivity to inputs when Y<bp. Strongly redistributes SA for threshold models.

inverse_sq.name: 1/(y^2+eps) (convex, even, regularised)
inverse_sq.physical_motivation: Inverse square law; gravitational/electric potential; Cauchy kernel.
inverse_sq.canonical_use_case: Near-singularity amplification study; heavy peak at y=0.
inverse_sq.notes: Even, convex everywhere (regularised). Amplifies near-zero outputs. Non-monotone.

iqr.name: Interquartile range (nonlocal spread)
iqr.physical_motivation: Interquartile range: robust spread measure; outlier-resistant scale estimator.
iqr.canonical_use_case: Robust spread SA; sensitivity of output variability to inputs.
iqr.notes: Nonlocal difference of quantiles. Ignores outliers. Robust alternative to variance.

legendre_p3.name: Legendre P3 polynomial
legendre_p3.physical_motivation: Legendre polynomial basis for spectral expansion; used in PCE sensitivity analysis.
legendre_p3.canonical_use_case: Probing non-monotone oscillatory redistribution; PCE-related SA studies.
legendre_p3.notes: Clipped to [-1,1] before application. Two zero crossings; three extrema.

log10_shift.name: Log base-10 (order-of-magnitude)
log10_shift.physical_motivation: Decadic logarithm; Richter scale; pH; orders-of-magnitude scaling.
log10_shift.notes: Same monotonicity as log_shift; rescales by 1/ln(10). Strong compression for large outputs.

log2_shift.name: Log base-2 (bit/octave scaling)
log2_shift.physical_motivation: Base-2 logarithm; information theory (bits); octave scaling in audio.
log2_shift.notes: Proportional to log-shift (natural); rescales by 1/ln(2). Same SA properties as log_shift.

log_auc.name: Log area-under-curve proxy
log_auc.physical_motivation: Log area-under-curve; pharmacokinetic exposure metric; bioavailability.
log_auc.canonical_use_case: PK/PD model SA; drug exposure analysis.
log_auc.notes: Log of sample mean. Nonlocal. Concave. Compresses large AUC values.

log_flow.name: Log streamflow (hydrological VST)
log_flow.physical_motivation: Log streamflow transform; standard in flood frequency and low-flow analysis.
log_flow.canonical_use_case: Hydrological model SA; flow duration curve analysis.
log_flow.notes: Same as log_shift but specific to streamflow context. Concave, monotone.

log_log.name: Double log log(1+log(y)) (extreme compr)
log_log.physical_motivation: Double logarithm; Gumbel distribution transform; extreme compression of heavy tails.
log_log.notes: Doubly concave. Very strong compression. Only monotone when inner log argument > 0.

log_logistic_cdf.name: Log-logistic CDF beta=2 (S-shaped tail)
log_logistic_cdf.physical_motivation: Log-logistic (Fisk) distribution CDF; survival analysis; income distributions.
log_logistic_cdf.canonical_use_case: Asymmetric S-shaped SA study with heavy right tail.
log_logistic_cdf.notes: S-shaped CDF. Heavy right tail for small beta. Monotone.

log_ratio.name: Log-ratio (ILR-like, mean-centred)
log_ratio.physical_motivation: Log-ratio (ILR-like) for compositional data; Aitchison geometry.
log_ratio.reference_full: Aitchison (1986). The Statistical Analysis of Compositional Data. Chapman & Hall.
log_ratio.notes: Mean-centred log-transform. Used in compositional data. Nonlocal (subtracts mean log).

mish.name: Mish activation (smooth non-monotone)
mish.physical_motivation: Mish activation; smooth non-monotone neural activation.
mish.notes: Similar to Swish. Non-monotone for y < ~-0.3. C-inf smooth everywhere.

modulus_lam05.name: Modulus transform lam=0.5 (C1 VST)
modulus_lam05.physical_motivation: Modulus transform (John & Draper 1980); extends Box-Cox to negative values.
modulus_lam05.canonical_use_case: General-purpose VST for signed data; lam=0.5 is square-root family.
modulus_lam05.notes: Monotone, C1 (not C2 at 0). Concave for y>0. Sign-preserving square root.

nash_sutcliffe.name: Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency proxy
nash_sutcliffe.physical_motivation: Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (NSE); standard hydrological model performance metric.
nash_sutcliffe.canonical_use_case: Hydrological model output SA; performance sensitivity.
nash_sutcliffe.notes: NSE=1 means perfect prediction. NSE<0 means model worse than mean. Nonlocal.

negentropy_proxy.name: Negentropy proxy (ICA contrast function)
negentropy_proxy.physical_motivation: Negentropy approximation (Hyvarinen 1998); ICA contrast function; departure from Gaussianity.
negentropy_proxy.canonical_use_case: SA of non-Gaussianity; information content sensitivity.
negentropy_proxy.notes: Fast negentropy proxy using moments. Nonlocal. High for heavy-tailed or skewed distributions.

pareto_tail.name: Pareto tail transform alpha=1.5
pareto_tail.physical_motivation: Power-law CDF; Pareto tail transform; generalised extreme-value tail behaviour.
pareto_tail.notes: alpha>1: concave (compresses upper tail). alpha<1: convex (amplifies upper tail). alpha=1: identity.

percentile_q10.name: 10th percentile (nonlocal quantile)
percentile_q10.physical_motivation: 10th percentile; lower tail quantile; minimum exceedance.
percentile_q10.canonical_use_case: Low-end SA; sensitivity to inputs driving low outputs.
percentile_q10.notes: Nonlocal quantile. Focuses SA on inputs that shift the lower tail.

percentile_q90.name: 90th percentile (nonlocal quantile)
percentile_q90.physical_motivation: 90th percentile; upper tail quantile; near-extreme threshold.
percentile_q90.notes: Nonlocal. Emphasises upper tail. Complements percentile_q10 for spread analysis.

poly4.name: y^4 (quartic, even, convex)
poly4.physical_motivation: Fourth-power loss functions; kurtosis weighting; sensitivity of extreme-output models.
poly4.canonical_use_case: Probing how SA indices shift under higher-order polynomial amplification.
poly4.reference_full: Hardy, G., Littlewood, J., Polya, G. (1934). Inequalities. Cambridge.
poly4.notes: Even, C-inf, strictly convex. All critical S1 structure erased for inputs whose conditional mean is near zero.

poly5.name: y^5 (quintic, odd, C-inf)
poly5.canonical_use_case: Higher-order curvature study; nonlinear amplification of tails.
poly5.notes: Odd, C-inf, monotone. Inflection at 0. Amplifies both tails asymmetrically when output is skewed.

poly6.name: y^6 (sextic, even, strictly convex)
poly6.physical_motivation: Sixth-power: extreme tail amplification, used in kurtosis analysis.

pot_log.name: Log peaks-over-threshold q=0.90
pot_log.physical_motivation: Log-transformed peaks-over-threshold; standard EVT for floods and extremes.
pot_log.canonical_use_case: Flood frequency SA; extreme precipitation analysis.
pot_log.reference_full: Coles (2001). An Introduction to Statistical Modeling of Extreme Values. Springer.
pot_log.notes: Combines threshold exceedance with log compression. Kills all non-exceedances.

power_exp.name: y^2*exp(-|y|) (hump, C-inf)
power_exp.physical_motivation: Gamma distribution kernel; hump-shaped response peaking at y=2/s.
power_exp.canonical_use_case: Non-monotone even hump SA; inputs producing moderate outputs most influential.
power_exp.notes: Even, C-inf. Peak at |y|=2/s. Zero at y=0. Suppresses both near-zero and extreme outputs.

quantile_delta.name: Quantile delta mapping q=0.90
quantile_delta.physical_motivation: Quantile delta mapping; bias correction for extreme quantiles in climate projections.
quantile_delta.notes: Scaling by upper quantile. Nonlocal. Emphasises exceedance structure.

ramp.name: Ramp/clip (piecewise linear, C0)
ramp.physical_motivation: Clamping/clipping; sensor saturation limits; bang-bang control deadband.
ramp.reference_full: Saltelli et al. (2008). Global Sensitivity Analysis.
ramp.notes: C0 at clip points. Affine (identity) in active region. Strong for tail-heavy outputs.

relative_abundance.name: Relative abundance (simplex projection)
relative_abundance.physical_motivation: Relative abundance; compositional data analysis (Aitchison); ecological proportions.
relative_abundance.notes: Simplex projection: outputs sum to 1. Nonlocal. Destroys absolute scale information.

renyi_entropy_a2.name: Renyi entropy alpha=2 (collision entropy)
renyi_entropy_a2.physical_motivation: Renyi entropy alpha=2 (collision entropy); more sensitive to distribution shape than Shannon entropy.
renyi_entropy_a2.canonical_use_case: SA of output distribution concentration; information content.
renyi_entropy_a2.reference_full: Renyi (1961). 4th Berkeley Symp Math Stat Prob.
renyi_entropy_a2.notes: Histogram-based: discretisation introduces C0 smoothness. alpha=2: log of inverse purity. Nonlocal.

safety_factor.name: Safety factor (inverse magnitude)
safety_factor.physical_motivation: Safety factor (factor of safety): capacity/demand ratio; structural and geotechnical engineering.
safety_factor.canonical_use_case: Structural SA; reliability under load uncertainty.
safety_factor.reference_full: Haldar & Mahadevan (2000). Probability, Reliability, and Statistical Methods.
safety_factor.notes: Inverts and compresses. Large outputs become small safety factors (dangerous). Non-monotone.

sample_kurtosis.name: Excess kurtosis (4th moment - 3)
sample_kurtosis.physical_motivation: Excess kurtosis: tail heaviness; fourth standardised cumulant minus 3.
sample_kurtosis.canonical_use_case: SA of tail behaviour; outlier sensitivity.
sample_kurtosis.notes: Normal distribution has kurtosis=0. Nonlocal. Extremely sensitive to extreme values.

sample_skewness.name: Sample skewness (3rd moment)
sample_skewness.physical_motivation: Sample skewness: asymmetry of output distribution; third standardised cumulant.
sample_skewness.notes: Very sensitive to outliers. Nonlocal. Can be negative (left tail).

sample_variance.name: Sample variance (nonlocal spread)
sample_variance.physical_motivation: Sample variance: spread of model output field; uncertainty quantification.
sample_variance.canonical_use_case: SA of output variability itself; meta-sensitivity.
sample_variance.reference_full: Fisher (1925). Statistical Methods for Research Workers.
sample_variance.notes: Nonlocal (uses second moment). Output is single scalar per sample. Quadratic sensitivity to extreme values.

sawtooth.name: Sawtooth wave (C0, periodic)
sawtooth.physical_motivation: Sawtooth wave; signal processing; modular arithmetic applications.
sawtooth.reference_full: Oppenheim & Schafer (1989). Discrete-Time Signal Processing. Prentice Hall.
sawtooth.notes: Jump discontinuities at each period. C-1 class. Strong SA redistribution expected.

selu.name: SELU activation (C1, piecewise)
selu.physical_motivation: Self-normalising activation (Klambauer et al.); preserves mean/variance during training.
selu.canonical_use_case: Piecewise monotone activation; C1 non-C2 study.
selu.notes: C1 but not C2 at y=0. Classified as nonsmooth (borderline). Monotone increasing.

sharpe_proxy.name: Sharpe ratio proxy (mean/std)
sharpe_proxy.physical_motivation: Sharpe ratio: risk-adjusted return; portfolio performance metric.
sharpe_proxy.notes: Nonlocal: depends on sample mean and std. Dimensionless. Strong SA redistribution.

signed_power_p05.name: Signed power p=0.5 (monotone, C1)
signed_power_p05.physical_motivation: Square-root compression with sign; generalisation of sqrt to all reals.
signed_power_p05.notes: Non-smooth at 0 (infinite derivative). Concave for y>0.

signed_power_p15.name: Signed power p=1.5 (monotone, C1)
signed_power_p15.physical_motivation: Smooth monotone power transform; used in Box-Cox family as p=1.5.
signed_power_p15.canonical_use_case: Variance stabilisation; moderately amplifying power law.
signed_power_p15.notes: Monotone, C1 (derivative = 1.5*|y|^0.5 continuous at 0). Mildly amplifies extremes.

sin_cos_product.name: sin*cos = 0.5*sin(2f) (harmonic)
sin_cos_product.physical_motivation: Harmonic product; second harmonic generation in nonlinear optics.

sin_squared.name: sin^2(fy) (bounded, non-negative)
sin_squared.physical_motivation: Power spectral density; squared oscillation; signal energy.
sin_squared.reference_full: Saltelli et al. (2010). Variance Based Sensitivity Analysis.
sin_squared.notes: Non-negative, bounded [0,1]. Mean=0.5. Even function. Equivalent to (1-cos(2fy))/2.

sinc.name: Normalised sinc (decaying oscillation)
sinc.physical_motivation: Sinc function; Fourier analysis; signal reconstruction.
sinc.notes: Numpy sinc is normalised. Decays as 1/|sy|. Many zero crossings.

soft_threshold.name: Soft threshold (lasso/shrinkage, C0)
soft_threshold.physical_motivation: LASSO soft-thresholding shrinkage; wavelet denoising operator.
soft_threshold.canonical_use_case: Sparse signal processing; sensitivity to near-zero outputs.
soft_threshold.notes: C0 at +-lambda. Kills inputs near zero. Strong SA redistribution for threshold-centred distributions.

softsign.name: Softsign y/(1+|y|) (monotone, C1)
softsign.physical_motivation: Softsign activation; algebraically bounded; slower saturation than tanh.
softsign.canonical_use_case: Bounded monotone with heavier polynomial tails than tanh.
softsign.notes: C1 but not C2 at y=0 (kink in derivative). Heavier tails than tanh.

spike_gaussian.name: Gaussian spike (C-inf, compact-ish)
spike_gaussian.physical_motivation: Gaussian bump/kernel; radial basis function; receptor tuning curves.
spike_gaussian.canonical_use_case: Peaked response SA study; sensitivity near a target value.
spike_gaussian.notes: Smooth. Focuses sensitivity on inputs producing Y near center. Strong SA redistribution.

square_wave.name: Square wave (discontinuous, periodic)
square_wave.physical_motivation: Square wave; bang-bang control; relay systems.
square_wave.notes: Binary output {-1,+1}. Jump discontinuities every half-period. Extreme SA redistribution.

std_precip_idx.name: Standardised precip index (Z-score)
std_precip_idx.physical_motivation: Standardised Precipitation Index (SPI); Z-score of precipitation anomaly.
std_precip_idx.canonical_use_case: Drought/flood index SA; standardised climate anomaly.
std_precip_idx.reference_full: McKee et al. (1993). 8th Conf. Applied Climatology.
std_precip_idx.notes: Z-score normalisation. Affine-like (within-sample standardisation). Near-commutative with SA.

stress_life.name: S-N Basquin curve (fatigue life)
stress_life.physical_motivation: Basquin S-N (stress-life) curve: fatigue life N=C*S^{-m}.
stress_life.canonical_use_case: Fatigue life SA; inverse power law sensitivity.
stress_life.notes: Strongly non-monotone in y (even). Decreasing in |y|. Amplifies sensitivity to inputs driving small loads.

swish.name: Swish activation (non-monotone)
swish.physical_motivation: Swish activation function from neural networks; non-monotone due to local minimum.
swish.canonical_use_case: Non-monotone smooth activation; SA under neural-network-like processing.
swish.notes: Non-monotone: has local minimum near y=-1.28 for beta=1. Mostly increasing for y>-1.28.

var_q95.name: VaR proxy q=0.95 (nonlocal quantile)
var_q95.physical_motivation: Value-at-Risk (VaR): loss not exceeded with 95% confidence; Basel III regulatory capital.
var_q95.canonical_use_case: Risk-measure SA; financial model tail analysis.
var_q95.notes: Quantile function: piecewise smooth. Nonlocal (collapses output field to single quantile). Strong SA redistribution.

von_mises_stress.name: Von Mises stress proxy (scaled |y|)
von_mises_stress.physical_motivation: Von Mises yield criterion; combined stress state; structural failure limit.
von_mises_stress.canonical_use_case: Structural reliability SA; failure criterion analysis.
von_mises_stress.notes: Scaled absolute value. Non-smooth at y=0. Even function. Loses sign information.

wasserstein_proxy.name: Wasserstein-1 proxy (MAD)
wasserstein_proxy.physical_motivation: Wasserstein-1 distance proxy (mean absolute deviation from median); optimal transport.
wasserstein_proxy.notes: Mean absolute deviation from median. Robust to outliers. Nonlocal.
